body_txt: The difference between courage and stupidity lies in the ability to react appropriately to fear: to know when to fight it and when to listen to it . Those who risk their lives for a worthy cause are brave; those who flee when they should stand are cowardly; those who risk their lives for a trivial gain are idiotic, and those who don't respond to fear at all are just plain fools. Feeling scared in a dangerous situation is the necessary condition for courage. Fearless Fools are never afraid, but that's not because they are braver than everyone else; they're not afraid because they don't know they're in danger , meaning their survival (if they do survive) will come down to blind luck . The opposite of this trope is the Dirty Coward , who is often scared but never brave. Also compare Miles Gloriosus , who are happy to talk about their bravery when they'll never be called on it, and Cowardly Lions , who never think their actions are brave because they have to fight their fear the entire time. Frequently the Aesop of Youth Is Wasted on the Dumb . Often a case of Attack! Attack! Attack! . May sing a Bravado Song . Compare Nobody Calls Me "Chicken"! and Suicidal Overconfidence . Also, compare Perilous Old Fool , who is frequently this. Contrast Fearless Undead , where reanimated dead beings — for obvious reasons — no longer have a survival instinct, and Fear-Induced Idiocy , for when fear makes people stupid. Naturally, any hero who blurs the line between 'brave' and 'stupid' is going to hear about it in the After-Action Patch-Up , even if they are ultimately praised for their deeds .
